1: ἁλληλούϊα — Alleluia: Hebrew hallelu Yah (praise YHWH); the only four occurrences in the NT are all in this chapter (Revelation 19:1, 3, 4, 6). The preservation of the Hebrew exclamation in a Greek text signals direct continuity with Israel's Passover liturgy (Pss 111–118, the Hallel psalms, were sung at Passover); the redeemed community in heaven joins the ancient liturgy of Israel. ἡ σωτηρία καί ἡ δόξα καί ἡ δύναμις τοῦ θεοῦ ἡμῶν — salvation and glory and power belong to our God: the threefold doxology grounds the heavenly rejoicing in divine attributes, not merely divine acts; these qualities belong essentially to God, not achieved by the angels' worship.
2: ἀληθιναί καί δίκαιαι αἱ κρίσεις αὐτοῦ — his judgments are true and just: alēthinos (genuine, ontologically real, not merely accurate) qualifies the judgments as corresponding to ultimate reality; dikaios (just, righteous) qualifies them as morally correct. Together they preempt any charge that God's judgment of Babylon was disproportionate, capricious, or mistaken. ἔκρινεν τήν πόρνην τήν μεγάλην — he has judged the great prostitute (aorist: the judgment is complete); the heavenly community attributes the fall of Rome directly to divine judicial action.
3: ὁ καπνός αὐτῆς ἀναβαίνει εἰς τοὺς αἰῶνας τῶν αἰώνων — her smoke goes up forever and ever: the permanence of Babylon's destruction; echoes Isaiah 34:9–10 (Edom's judgment: the smoke "goes up forever" and "from generation to generation it shall lie waste"). The eternal smoke is not an eternal burning but the permanent evidence of completed judgment — the site of empire becomes perpetually marked desolation. The second Hallelujah confirms and extends the first.
4: ἔπεσαν οἱ πρεσβύτεροι οἱ εἴκοσι τέσσαρες καί τά τέσσαρα ζῷα — the twenty-four elders and four living creatures fell down (aorist; the prostration is total and immediate): the same heavenly court that has witnessed all the seal, trumpet, and bowl judgments now worships in response to Babylon's fall. ἀμήν καί ἁλληλούϊα — Amen and Alleluia: the Hebrew Amen (so be it; affirmation of truth) combined with hallelu Yah creates the liturgical double affirmation; the elders and living creatures ratify the heavenly chorus's praise.
5: φωνή ἀπό τοῦ θρόνου — a voice from the throne: authoritative but unidentified; not Christ (he does not call himself a fellow-servant with John in Revelation 19:10) and not the Father (the community is told to praise "our God"), so most likely an angel of the throne. αἰνεῖτε τῷ θεῷ ἡμῶν πάντες οἱ δοῦλοι αὐτοῦ — praise our God, all his servants: the dative tō theō (to/for God) directs the praise toward its object; doulos (slave/servant) is the highest designation the redeemed bear — Paul's (Romans 1:1), John's (Revelation 1:1), and Christ's servants' shared identity.
6: ὡς φωνήν ὄχλου πολλοῦ καί ὡς φωνήν ὑδάτων πολλῶν καί ὡς φωνήν βροντῶν ἰσχυρῶν — like the voice of a great multitude and like the sound of many waters and like the sound of mighty thunders: the triple simile echoes the voice of YHWH in Ezekiel 1:24; 43:2 and the glorified Christ in Revelation 1:15; all three are combined, signaling that the praise of the redeemed carries divine authority. ἐβασίλευσεν κύριος ὁ θεός ὁ παντοκράτωρ — the Lord God Almighty has begun to reign (ingressive aorist: has become king, not "became king" but the moment of universal acknowledgment of what always was true); after Babylon's fall the divine reign is manifest.
7: ὁ γάμος τοῦ ἀρνίου ἦλθεν — the wedding of the Lamb has come (aorist: arrived, is here): the marriage metaphor pervades the OT covenant relationship (Isaiah 54:5–6; Jeremiah 2:2; Ezek 16; Hosea 2:19–20 — YHWH as husband of Israel); the Lamb marries his bride = the covenant relationship reaches its eschatological consummation. ἡτοίμασεν ἑαυτήν — she prepared herself (reflexive heautēn): the bride's preparation is her own active response; the passive edothē (it was given to her) in Revelation 19:8 preserves divine initiative, but the preparedness is the bride's genuine own action within that gift.
8: βύσσινον λαμπρόν καθαρόν — fine linen bright and pure: the bridal garment of the church directly contrasts with the prostitute's dress (Revelation 17:4: purple and scarlet; Revelation 18:16). The fabric is the same (byssinon = fine linen appears in Revelation 18:12, 16 of Babylon's garments) but the moral status is opposite: the church's linen is bright and pure, the prostitute's is stained with blood and abomination. τά δικαιώματα τῶν ἁγίων — the righteous deeds/acts of the saints: dikaiōma (righteous deed, requirement fulfilled); the garment is woven from the saints' actual righteous acts — not credited righteousness but enacted righteousness, the fruit of the Spirit in the community.
9: μακάριοι οἱ εἰς τό δεῖπνον τοῦ γάμου τοῦ ἀρνίου κεκλημένοι — blessed are those called to the marriage supper of the Lamb: the fourth beatitude in Revelation (Revelation 1:3; Revelation 14:13; Revelation 16:15; Revelation 19:9); keklēmenoi = those who have been called (perfect passive participle: the completed divine calling with ongoing present state of being-called). The marriage supper stands in deliberate contrast to the "great supper of God" (Revelation 19:17), where the carrion birds feast on the slain armies: one supper is the feast of redemption, the other the feast of judgment. οὗτοι οἱ λόγοι ἀληθινοί τοῦ θεοῦ εἰσίν — these are the true/genuine words of God: the angelic affirmation uses alēthinos (ontologically real, not merely accurate); the vision corresponds to ultimate reality.
10: σύνδουλός σού εἰμι — I am your fellow slave: the angel's refusal of worship (hora mē = watch out, stop it!) establishes the absolute distinction between creature and Creator; however exalted, the angel is a doulos (slave) as John is. ἡ γάρ μαρτυρία Ἰησοῦ ἐστιν τό πνεῦμα τῆς προφητείας — for the testimony of Jesus is the spirit of prophecy: the genitive Iēsou is most likely both subjective (the testimony Jesus himself gives — he is the chief prophet, the Word of God, Revelation 19:13) and objective (testimony about Jesus — the content that all genuine prophecy aims at); all true prophecy has Jesus as its source and its subject.
11: ἠνεῳγμένον τόν οὐρανόν — heaven opened (perfect passive participle: the state of being-open following a completed opening; the heavenly realm stands permanently accessible for this final revelation). λεγόμενος Πιστός καί Ἀληθινός — called Faithful and True: the rider's first named title; pistos (faithful, trustworthy) echoes Revelation 3:14 (Jesus the "faithful and true witness"); alēthinos (genuine, the real thing) distinguishes him from all pretenders who claim divine authority. ἐν δικαιοσύνῃ κρίνει καί πολεμεῖ — in righteousness he judges and makes war: dikaiosynē governs both the judicial and military action; the warfare is just precisely because it is judicial, not arbitrary conquest.
12: ὀφθαλμοί αὐτοῦ ὡς φλόξ πυρός — his eyes like a flame of fire: repeated from the Son-of-Man vision of Revelation 1:14; the divine scrutiny that penetrates all pretense and self-deception. στεφάνους πολλούς — many diadems: diadēma (royal diadem, the headband of sovereignty) rather than stephanos (victor's wreath); the many diadems against the dragon's seven (Revelation 12:3) and the beast's ten (Revelation 13:1) — Christ's crown-count exceeds all rivals. ὄνομα γεγραμμένον ὃ οὐδεῖς οἶδεν εἰ μή αὐτός — a name written which no one knew except himself: the divine name beyond human language; in OT tradition, knowing a being's true name meant mastery over it (Genesis 32:29; Exodus 3:13–14); Christ's name exceeds human comprehension and cannot be controlled by creature-knowledge.
13: ῥεραντισμένον αἵματι — sprinkled with blood: the majority manuscript reading from rhantizō (to sprinkle/besprinkle) vs. bebammenon (dipped, from baptō) in a minority of manuscripts. The "sprinkled" reading is to be preferred as the harder reading and as the more theologically loaded: the high priest sprinkled blood on the Day of Atonement (Leviticus 16:14–15); Isaiah 52:15 uses "sprinkle" for the Servant's action on many nations. The blood on the robe may be the rider's own atoning blood — worn before any battle, identifying him as the Lamb before he is the warrior. ὁ Λόγος τοῦ θεοῦ — the Word of God: the only occurrence of the Logos title outside the Johannine prologue (John 1:1, 14); in the Apocalypse, the Word of God is the active divine speech that creates, judges, and renews — the rider is divine speech made visible and armed.
14: τά στρατεύματα τά ἐν τῷ οὐρανῷ ἠκολούθει αὐτῷ — the armies in heaven were following him (imperfect: ongoing march, the sustained movement of the heavenly host). ἐνδεδυμένοι βύσσινον λευκόν καθαρόν — clothed in fine linen white and pure: the heavenly armies wear the same garment as the bride (Revelation 19:8, same words byssinon, katharon); the armies and the bride are the same community viewed from different perspectives — the church is both the wedding party and the host that follows the rider.
15: ἐκπορεύεται ῥομφαία ὀξεῖα — a sharp sword goes out (present: the ongoing reality of divine speech-as-weapon); rhomphaia (the large Thracian broadsword) rather than machaira (short dagger); the sword from the mouth (Revelation 1:16; Revelation 2:12, 16) identifies the warfare as by the word of God (Isaiah 11:4; Revelation 49:2; Hebrews 4:12 — the word sharper than any two-edged sword). ποιμανεῖ αὐτούς ἐν ῥάβδῳ σιδηρᾷ — he will shepherd them with an iron rod (Psalms 2:9): poimainō (to shepherd) is unexpected in a battle context — governing and shepherding cannot be separated for the Lamb-who-is-Lion. πατεῖ τήν ληνόν τοῦ οἴνου τοῦ θυμοῦ τῆς ὀργῆς τοῦ θεοῦ τοῦ παντοκράτορος — treads the winepress of the wine of the fury of the wrath of God Almighty: the accumulation of genitives conveys rhetorical intensity; the winepress of divine judgment from Isaiah 63:3 and Joel 3:13 is trodden by the rider himself.
16: ἐπί τόν μηρόν αὐτοῦ — on his thigh: the warrior straps his sword to his thigh (Psalms 45:3: "Gird your sword on your thigh, O warrior"); the name is inscribed where the weapon hangs, uniting his sovereign title and his fighting posture. Βασιλεύς βασιλέων καί Κύριος κυρίων — King of kings and Lord of lords: the title first attributed to the Lamb at 17:14 is now visibly inscribed on the rider's robe; YHWH is "God of gods and Lord of lords" in Deuteronomy 10:17 and Daniel 2:47. The rider bears the exclusive divine superlative as his war-name, the counter-claim to every imperial title.
17: ἑστῶτα ἐν τῷ ἡλίῳ — standing in the sun: the angel's position in the source of all natural light signals maximal visibility and authority; the great supper announcement is made at cosmic volume. εἰς τό δεῖπνον τό μέγα τοῦ θεοῦ — to the great supper of God: the deliberate anti-type to the marriage supper of the Lamb (Revelation 19:9); both are feasts, but one is the feast of redemption and the other the feast of judgment (from Ezekiel 39:17–20, the great sacrificial feast on the mountains of Israel after the defeat of Gog).
18: σάρκας βασιλέων ... σάρκας χιλιάρχων ... σάρκας ἰσχυρῶν — flesh of kings, flesh of commanders, flesh of the mighty: the social order is completely inverted; those who ordered the deaths of the saints are themselves food for carrion birds. ἐλευθέρων τε καί δούλων — free and slave: the social distinction that structured Roman life (free, freed, slave — the three legal categories) is irrelevant in divine judgment; every social rank is equally subject to the rider's word.
19: συνηγμένα ποιῆσαι τόν πόλεμον μετά τοῦ καθημένου ἐπί τοῦ ἵππου — gathered to make war against the rider on the horse (perfect passive participle synēgmena: the state of having been gathered, implying a prior act of gathering): the passive voice asks "by whom?" and implies divine agency in summoning even the beast's final coalition. The gathering of nations against YHWH and his anointed (Psalms 2:1–2) and the Gog-Magog oracle (Ezek 38–39) both underlie this final confrontation.
20: ἐπιάσθη τό θηρίον — the beast was captured (aorist passive: a single completed divine act); no battle is described, no struggle narrated; the capture is absolute and immediate. ὁ ψευδοπροφήτης ὁ ποιήσας τά σημεῖα ἐνώπιον αὐτοῦ — the false prophet who performed the signs before it: the beast of Revelation 13:11–17 is here definitively identified as a false prophet; his signs are exposed as deception, not divine authentication. ζῶντες ἐβλήθησαν — they were thrown alive (without death, without the intermediate state): the immediate transition to the lake of fire without passing through death and Hades marks their judgment as categorically different from the general judgment of chapter 20.
21: ἀπεκτάνθησαν ἐν τῇ ῥομφαίᾳ τοῦ καθημένου ἐπί τοῦ ἵππου τῇ ἐξελθούσῃ ἐκ τοῦ στόματος αὐτοῦ — were killed by the sword of the one seated on the horse, the sword going out of his mouth: the instrument of judgment is specified — not a physical military weapon but the word of Christ (the rhomphaia from his mouth, Revelation 19:15); the victory is accomplished by divine speech before any conventional battle is joined. ἐχορτάσθησαν πάντα τά ὄρνεα ἐκ τῶν σαρκῶν αὐτῶν — all the birds were gorged on their flesh (echortasthēsan = were filled to satisfaction, aorist passive of chortazō): the prophetic bird-feast of Ezekiel 39:17–20 is fulfilled; Gog's armies are consumed in the very manner the oracle promised.
